critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with generally sound methodology. Let me work through the genuine concerns systematically.

**Issue 1: Random split across calendar time creates a temporal leakage analog specific to EHR data**

The specific claim: stratifying randomly across the full 2020–2023 cohort and splitting 70/15/15 ensures both models are evaluated fairly with no distributional advantage.

Why this might be wrong: EHR data has a structural property that random splits don't address — temporal concept drift in coding practice, formulary changes, and institutional protocol changes. More concretely, the LSTM trained on 2020–2023 discharges will have seen 2022 lab trajectories in training; when it encounters 2022 lab trajectories in the test set (from the same period, just different patients), it has implicitly 'learned' the distributional fingerprint of each calendar era. A static model has less to memorize here because its features are lower-dimensional summaries. The LSTM may be learning temporal epoch-specific patterns (e.g., COVID-era creatinine distributions, a formulary switch for diuretics) that are present in both train and test by design, creating an asymmetric advantage that would not hold in prospective deployment. This is not the same as label leakage but it is a form of temporal co-contamination that inflates generalization estimates for sequence models more than for tabular ones.

Evidence that would resolve this: compare results under random split vs. a strict temporal holdout (train on 2020–2022, test on 2023). If the LSTM advantage shrinks substantially under the temporal holdout, the random-split advantage is partly artifactual. The design mentions this is out of scope for generalization claims, but the absence of any temporal split makes the within-sample comparison somewhat favorable to the more expressive model.

**Issue 2: The sensitivity analysis for feature parity is listed as optional ('may refit'), but it is actually load-bearing for the core hypothesis**

The specific claim: the longitudinal model's superiority, if observed, reflects sequence representation rather than merely richer temporal data access.

Why this might be wrong: the baseline receives Charlson index, prior 12-month utilization, and discharge disposition — all static. The longitudinal model receives, in addition, lab slopes/ranges over 14 and 30 days, medication trajectories, and vital sign trends. A gradient-boosted model trained on those same engineered aggregates (slopes, ranges, missingness flags, medication counts) would capture much of the same predictive signal without any sequence model. The design acknowledges this in Section 7 but frames the feature-parity sensitivity analysis as optional. If the sequence model outperforms the static baseline but a GBM with the same temporal aggregates matches or exceeds the sequence model, the conclusion 'sequence representation adds value' is false — it is actually 'temporal aggregates add value.' The core hypothesis is about representation form (sequential vs. tabular), not data access. Without the feature-parity arm being mandatory rather than optional, the experiment cannot distinguish these two hypotheses.

Evidence: make the temporal-aggregate GBM baseline a required comparison arm. If the LSTM beats static-only but not temporal-aggregate GBM, the experiment's stated conclusion is not supported.

**Issue 3: AUPRC as primary metric is systematically sensitive to class imbalance estimation from the training set, creating a subtle calibration-inflation risk for the more flexible model**

The specific claim: AUPRC is the appropriate primary metric for this clinical use case and will fairly rank the two models.

Why this might be wrong: AUPRC is computed against observed readmission labels, but 90-day readmission rates in EHR cohorts typically range from 12–20% depending on exclusions. With ~35k val+test patients and ~15–20% outcome rate, AUPRC values will be moderately stable. However, AUPRC is sensitive to the effective positive rate in the specific test partition. Because the split is random and stratified on readmission status, this is controlled — so this concern is minor here. The more genuine issue is that AUPRC rewards models that concentrate probability mass at the top of the risk distribution. The LSTM, with attention over 30-day sequences, has more degrees of freedom to overfit this concentration on training data in ways that inflate apparent AUPRC on in-distribution random splits relative to prospective deployment. This is the same mechanism as issue 1 but expressed through the metric: AUPRC is more sensitive than AUROC to overfit rank-ordering at the extremes.

Evidence: compare AUPRC on training set vs. val vs. test for each model; a large train-to-test gap in AUPRC for the LSTM relative to the baseline would flag this. The design does not mention this diagnostic.

**Issue 4: Censoring treatment is inconsistent with using AUPRC as primary metric**

The specific claim: patients censored before 90 days are handled correctly, and AUPRC is an appropriate primary metric given this censoring structure.

Why this might be wrong: AUPRC as implemented here treats censored patients as negatives (no readmission observed within 90 days = label 0). If administrative censoring (end of observation window, December 2023) is non-random with respect to readmission risk — which it almost certainly is, since late-2023 discharges have shorter observation windows — then the 'negative' labels for censored patients are contaminated: some fraction of them would have been readmitted had follow-up continued. The design mentions 'censoring at 90 days or end of observation window' but then uses a binary AUPRC rather than a survival-adjusted discrimination metric. Harrell's C-index is listed as secondary and would handle this correctly, but AUPRC cannot. The magnitude of bias depends on the proportion of the cohort with truncated observation windows (late 2023 discharges) and the censoring mechanism. If both models are equally affected, relative comparisons hold — but if the longitudinal model is better calibrated on late-2023 discharges (perhaps because it saw fewer of them in training too), the direction of bias is asymmetric.

Evidence: report the proportion of the cohort with observation windows shorter than 90 days (discharged after October 2023). If this exceeds ~5% of the test set, run a sensitivity analysis restricting evaluation to discharges with full 90-day follow-up windows and check whether the primary metric results change.

**Summary assessment:**

Issue 2 (the optional feature-parity arm) is the most fundamental flaw — it makes the core hypothesis untestable as designed. The experiment could conclude 'LSTM beats static baseline' while being unable to determine whether this is due to sequence modeling or temporal feature richness. This is not a minor concern; it undermines the experiment's stated purpose.

Issue 1 (random vs. temporal split) is a genuine threat to the validity of the within-study generalization estimate, though the scope of claims explicitly limits conclusions to this health system and period.

Issue 4 (censoring/AUPRC mismatch) is a real but quantifiable threat that could be addressed with a simple sensitivity analysis.

Issue 3 is a weaker concern given the stratified split; I would not weight it heavily.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Anticipated Criticism 1: Temporal Split Omitted — Random Split Inflates Performance for Both Models

**Claim:** A stratified random split across the full cohort violates temporal independence. Discharges from the same patient appear in both train and test; temporal autocorrelation in coding practices, hospital policies, and population health trends (COVID, seasonal illness) artificially inflates both models' performance estimates, and more importantly may bias the *comparison* if the longitudinal model exploits temporal autocorrelation more than the static model.

**Validity Assessment:** This is a genuine and significant concern. The design acknowledges 'no patient-level leakage' (each discharge is a separate instance), but this is not the same as temporal independence. A patient with three discharges can have two in training and one in test. More critically, the random split means the test set's calendar distribution mirrors the training set's — there is no evaluation of forward-generalization. For a longitudinal model that learns temporal trajectory patterns, if those patterns shift over a 4-year window (e.g., post-COVID lab value distributions, formulary changes affecting medication trajectories), the performance estimate from a random split will be optimistic relative to prospective deployment. The static model, being simpler, may be less sensitive to this distributional shift, meaning the *gap* between models could be overestimated in either direction.

**Evidence:** The design does not include a temporal holdout or time-based sensitivity analysis. The 2020–2023 window spans significant healthcare disruption (COVID-19 waves, staffing crises, telehealth policy shifts) that would cause non-stationarity in EHR-derived trajectories.

**Genuine Uncertainty:** Whether the bias from random splitting favors the longitudinal or static model is genuinely unclear — it could go either way. This is a meaningful threat to the validity of the comparison claim.

**Concession Required:** This is a real flaw. The design should include a temporal holdout analysis (e.g., train on 2020–2022, test on 2023) as a sensitivity check. Without it, claims of superiority are not fully defensible for prospective deployment. However, the flaw is *scoped*: the design explicitly limits claims to 'this health system and time period,' which partially absorbs this concern, though not fully since the random split still conflates past and future.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 2: Patient-Level Leakage Despite Discharge-Level Indexing

**Claim:** Multiple discharges from the same patient appearing in both training and test creates patient-level information leakage. The model may learn patient-specific patterns (lab baselines, medication regimens, care team behaviors) rather than generalizable clinical trajectories.

**Validity Assessment:** Valid concern, especially for the longitudinal model. If a patient's creatinine trajectory is highly idiosyncratic, seeing their earlier discharges in training effectively teaches the model that particular patient's risk profile. For the static model this matters less, but for an LSTM with attention over lab/medication sequences, it is a meaningful concern.

**Evidence:** The design states 'each discharge is a separate prediction instance' and that 'train/val/test cohorts do not overlap' at the discharge level. This is technically correct but mischaracterizes the leakage risk, which is at the patient level, not the discharge level.

**Genuine Uncertainty:** In a large multi-hospital cohort of 180k–220k discharges, the proportion of patients with multiple encounters is non-trivial (likely 20–40% in a real health system). The magnitude of bias introduced is uncertain without knowing the repeat-encounter rate.

**Concession Required:** This is a real limitation. The appropriate fix is patient-level splitting (all discharges from a given patient go to one partition). The design does not implement this. For the stated scope — comparing longitudinal vs. static representation — this leakage may disproportionately benefit the longitudinal model if repeat patients provide trajectory autocorrelation that the LSTM exploits.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 3: AUPRC as Primary Metric Is Sensitive to Prevalence and May Not Favor Longitudinal Models for the Right Reasons

**Claim:** AUPRC is prevalence-dependent and harder to interpret across subgroups. At typical 90-day readmission rates (~15–25%), AUPRC values will be moderate, and differences may be statistically meaningful but clinically negligible.

**Validity Assessment:** Partially valid. The design's rationale for AUPRC — focusing on the high-risk segment relevant to intervention capacity — is sound and well-justified. The inclusion of Net Benefit at clinical thresholds (20%, 30%, 40%) as a secondary metric is appropriate and partially addresses this. However, the design does not specify how statistical significance of AUPRC differences will be assessed (bootstrap CIs? DeLong-equivalent for AUPRC?).

**Evidence:** The metric suite is comprehensive (AUPRC, C-index, calibration, net benefit, stratified AUPRC). The absence of explicit confidence interval methodology is a gap.

**Genuine Uncertainty:** Whether observed AUPRC differences will be clinically significant vs. merely statistically significant is unknown a priori. This is not a design flaw per se — it is a result-dependent question — but the absence of pre-specified minimum clinically important difference (MCID) is a weakness.

**Concession:** Minor. The design is stronger than typical for metric selection and multi-metric reporting. The missing piece is explicit CI methodology and MCID specification.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 4: The LSTM/GRU May Not Be the Right Architecture — Transformer or Simpler Aggregation May Dominate

**Claim:** The methodology allows 'alternatively, a gradient-boosted model with manually-engineered temporal aggregates,' which effectively conflates two different hypotheses: (a) sequence models vs. static models, and (b) neural vs. tree-based models. The alternative arm muddies the comparison.

**Validity Assessment:** This is a fair critique. The design's flexibility in allowing XGBoost/LightGBM with engineered aggregates as an acceptable 'longitudinal model' means the experiment could end up testing feature engineering richness rather than sequence representation. However, the Section 7 sensitivity analysis note ('refit the baseline with manually-engineered temporal aggregates to confirm that longitudinal model gains come from sequence representation') partially addresses this — it acknowledges the confound and proposes a way to disentangle it.

**Evidence:** The sensitivity analysis is described as 'may refit' — it is not mandatory. This weakens the design's ability to make the specific claim about sequence representation value.

**Genuine Uncertainty:** Without pre-committing to the LSTM/GRU arm as the primary longitudinal model, the interpretation of results is ambiguous.

**Concession:** Moderate. The 'alternatively acceptable' framing should be tightened: either (a) commit to the LSTM/GRU as primary with XGBoost-with-aggregates as an additional ablation, or (b) reframe the claim as 'richer temporal features' rather than 'sequence representation.' The current framing is loose.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 5: The Longitudinal Model Has a Feature Advantage Beyond Sequence Architecture

**Claim:** The longitudinal model receives all static features *plus* temporal trajectories. The comparison is confounded: differences in performance could reflect more information, not better representation.

**Validity Assessment:** This is a legitimate and classic confound in longitudinal vs. static comparisons. The design acknowledges this in Section 7 ('sensitivity analysis may refit the baseline with manually-engineered temporal aggregates'). However, this sensitivity analysis is optional ('may'), and the temporal aggregates that would be added to the baseline are not fully specified.

**Evidence:** Section 3 explicitly states the longitudinal model receives 'all baseline features' plus temporal features. The baseline does include 'prior 12-month utilization' which captures some longitudinal signal. However, lab slopes, vital trends, and medication trajectories are strictly additive information for the longitudinal arm.

**Concession:** The design partially addresses this but not rigorously enough. The sensitivity analysis needs to be mandatory and the temporal aggregates need to be pre-specified for the comparison to isolate sequence representation. Without this, 'the value of sequence representation' cannot be claimed — only 'the value of richer temporal features,' which is a weaker and different claim. This is a meaningful gap.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 6: Calibration and Recalibration Asymmetry

**Claim:** Platt scaling is applied to the neural model but not explicitly stated for the baseline. This creates an asymmetry in calibration treatment.

**Validity Assessment:** The design states calibration is applied via Platt scaling on the neural longitudinal model. Logistic regression and gradient-boosted baselines may or may not require recalibration depending on implementation. The design does not specify whether the baseline receives equivalent recalibration treatment.

**Evidence:** Minor gap. Gradient-boosted models are known to be poorly calibrated without post-hoc recalibration. If the baseline GBM is not recalibrated and the neural model is, calibration slope comparisons will be unfair.

**Concession:** Small but real. Should specify that all models receive the same post-hoc calibration procedure.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 7: 90-Day Outcome Window and Censoring Treatment

**Claim:** Patients discharged near the end of the observation window (December 2023) will have truncated follow-up, creating informative censoring if late-period patients differ systematically from early-period patients.

**Validity Assessment:** Valid concern. The design acknowledges censoring but treats it as 'naturally-occurring' and non-informative. In a random-split design that mixes calendar time across train/test, patients censored due to end-of-window will be distributed across all partitions — this is consistent with the random split but does not make the censoring non-informative. If December 2023 discharges have different risk profiles (e.g., post-COVID comorbidity burden), the censored patients may be systematically different.

**Evidence:** The design uses C-index as a secondary metric, which appropriately accounts for censoring. The primary AUPRC metric treats the outcome as a binary label at 90 days, which may mishandle patients with <90 days of follow-up.

**Genuine Uncertainty:** The proportion of patients with <90 days of observation time and their systematic differences from fully-observed patients is unknown.

**Concession:** Moderate. The binary label treatment of AUPRC for censored patients is a limitation. The design should either exclude patients with <90 days of observation (biasing toward patients discharged before October 2023) or use a survival-based metric consistently. The current approach mixes binary and survival frameworks in a way that could introduce bias.

---

### Summary of Pass 1

I've identified the following issues by severity:

**Critical (threaten the validity of the core comparison claim):**
1. Random split without patient-level partitioning — patient-level leakage disproportionately favors the longitudinal model
2. No temporal holdout — performance estimates are not prospectively valid
3. Feature advantage confound — mandatory temporal aggregate sensitivity analysis is missing; the core claim 'sequence representation adds value' cannot be established without it

**Moderate (limit interpretability but don't fully invalidate):**
4. Loose definition of 'longitudinal model' — LSTM vs. XGBoost-with-aggregates conflation
5. AUPRC/censoring treatment inconsistency for end-of-window patients
6. Calibration asymmetry across models

**Minor (addressable gaps):**
7. No pre-specified MCID or CI methodology for AUPRC differences

The three critical issues together mean that the experiment, as designed, cannot reliably establish the specific claim in Section 8: 'whether an RNN-based longitudinal model achieves better concentration of 90-day readmission risk than a static tabular baseline.' The patient-level leakage and feature advantage confound are design flaws that would need to be fixed, not just acknowledged. The temporal split issue affects external validity.

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Three critical issues have been identified through honest analysis:
- Patient-level leakage in the split strategy that is not acknowledged in the design and disproportionately favors the longitudinal model
- Absence of mandatory feature-parity sensitivity analysis, meaning the core comparison claim is confounded by information quantity vs. representation quality
- No temporal holdout, limiting prospective validity claims

These are not minor calibration issues or prototype limitations — they go to the heart of whether the experimental comparison is valid. The design is well-specified in many respects (preprocessing discipline, metric suite, confound awareness, scope limitation), but the three critical flaws are not remedied by the existing design elements.

The appropriate verdict is **critique_wins**: the methodology has critical unaddressed flaws that undermine the primary comparative claim, and these flaws require redesign (patient-level splits, mandatory temporal aggregate ablation, temporal holdout sensitivity analysis), not just empirical testing of an otherwise sound design.
all_issues_raised: Random split across calendar time creates asymmetric temporal co-contamination: the LSTM may learn era-specific distributional fingerprints present in both train and test, inflating its apparent advantage over the static model relative to prospective performance.

The feature-parity sensitivity analysis (temporal-aggregate GBM vs. LSTM) is listed as optional but is load-bearing for the core hypothesis — without it, the experiment cannot distinguish 'sequence representation adds value' from 'temporal aggregates add value.'

AUPRC as primary metric rewards rank-ordering concentration at distribution extremes, where the more flexible LSTM has greater capacity to overfit in-distribution random splits; train-to-test AUPRC gap diagnostics are absent.

Treating administratively censored patients as clean negatives in AUPRC computation is inconsistent with the censoring structure; late-2023 discharges with truncated observation windows will have contaminated labels, and the magnitude and direction of bias may differ between models.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random split across calendar time creates asymmetric temporal co-contamination: the LSTM may learn era-specific distributional fingerprints present in both train and test, inflating its apparent advantage over the static model relative to prospective performance — defender conceded this as a real flaw requiring a temporal holdout sensitivity analysis.

The feature-parity sensitivity analysis (temporal-aggregate GBM vs. LSTM) is listed as optional but is load-bearing for the core hypothesis — defender conceded this is a meaningful gap and that the sensitivity analysis must be mandatory and pre-specified for the experiment to isolate sequence representation value.

Treating administratively censored patients (late-2023 discharges with truncated observation windows) as clean negatives in AUPRC computation is inconsistent with the censoring structure — defender conceded this as a moderate limitation requiring either exclusion of under-observed patients or a survival-based primary metric.

Patient-level leakage from multiple discharges per patient across train/test partitions (raised by defender in Pass 1, not in original critic analysis) was conceded as a real flaw that disproportionately favors the longitudinal model and requires patient-level splitting.
